title: Epilepsy — Chronic Management
oneLiner: Chronic epilepsy management in adults – selecting & monitoring antiseizure medications (ASMs) to achieve seizure freedom while minimising adverse effects and risks.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Status epilepticus** – prolonged seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery. **Key finding:** ongoing convulsive activity or unresponsive after 10 min → ABC, IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once after 10 min), call senior anaesthetics/ICU.  
- **Abrupt AED withdrawal** – sudden cessation of any ASM (especially benzodiazepines, phenytoin, valproate) can precipitate life-threatening seizures. **Key finding:** patient reports stopping medication in last 48–72 hours, with low or absent drug levels.  
- **AED toxicity** – e.g. phenytoin overdose causing ataxia, nystagmus, encephalopathy → check drug levels urgently; may need dose reduction or charcoal if massive overdose.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Recurrent, unprovoked seizures (focal aware/impaired awareness, generalised tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic)  
  - Breakthrough seizures despite therapy (trigger: missed doses, sleep deprivation, illness, new drug interaction)  
  - Side effects of current AED: sedation, dizziness, tremor, rash, weight gain, mood changes  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Post-ictal confusion, Todd’s paresis, tongue or cheek lacerations, urinary incontinence  
  - In between seizures: usually normal neuro exam; may have subtle bradypsychia or ataxia from AEDs
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:**  
  - Epilepsy (multiple seizure types, EEG/MRI supportive)  
  - Syncope (brief loss of consciousness, injury rare, no post-ictal confusion, usually provoked)  
- **Less likely:**  
  - Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) – sustained eye closure, pelvic thrusting, no post-ictal state, no EEG correlate  
  - Transient ischaemic attack (TIA) – negative symptoms (weakness/numbness), no rhythmic jerking, no loss of consciousness  
  - Arrhythmia (e.g. long QT, Wolff-Parkinson-White) – syncope during exercise, family history, ECG abnormal  
- **Splitter:** Tongue bite (epilepsy vs. PNES) – lateral tongue bite >2mm strongly suggests true convulsion; PNES rarely causes bite.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimic)  
  - ECG (arrhythmia if syncope suspect)  
  - Urine pregnancy test (if woman of childbearing age – mandatory before starting valproate)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, Ca/Mg/PO4 (electrolyte derangement)  
  - AED levels (only if toxicity or adherence concern – check trough levels for phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate)  
  - ESR/CRP (if suspect CNS infection)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** MRI brain (epilepsy protocol – detects hippocampal sclerosis, tumours, malformations)  
  - **Second-line:** CT head (if MRI contraindicated or acute trauma/haemorrhage suspected)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - EEG (outpatient, ideally within 24–72 hours of seizure – shows interictal epileptiform discharges)  
  - Prolonged video-EEG if uncertain diagnosis or surgical candidacy  
- **Never-miss test:** **AED level** (before changing dose) + **pregnancy test** (in all women of childbearing age starting or changing AED, especially valproate)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If active seizure: ABC, IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once if still seizing after 10 min); if ongoing → phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV or levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV [check local policy]  
  - Identify and treat underlying trigger: hypoglycaemia, infections, drug withdrawal, missed doses  
  - Check AED levels (if known drug, e.g. phenytoin)  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Reconcile all ASMs – ensure same drug, dose, interval as outpatient (or adjust if toxicity)  
  - Establish seizure diary and triggers (sleep, alcohol, missed doses)  
  - Start new ASM if treatment-naïve or switching: **first-line monotherapy**  
    - Focal seizures: lamotrigine (slow titration to reduce rash risk) or levetiracetam  
    - Generalised tonic-clonic: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or valproate (avoid women of childbearing potential)  
    - Absence: ethosuximide or valproate  
    - Myoclonic: valproate or levetiracetam  
  - Check baseline LFTs, FBC, renal function  
  - Educate patient/carer: medication adherence, driving regulations (DVLA: must stop driving for 12 months after first seizure or 6 months if controlled), SUDEP risk counselling  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Titrate to maintenance dose over weeks (see titration schedules in BNF/Antiepileptic drug guidelines)  
  - Monitor for adverse effects: rash (lamotrigine), behavioural change (levetiracetam), weight gain (valproate, gabapentin), osteoporosis (enzyme-inducers: carbamazepine, phenytoin)  
  - Annual review: seizure control, side effects, blood tests if on enzyme-inducing AED (bone profile, vitamin D)  
  - Consider drug level monitoring if non-adherence, toxicity risk, or pregnancy planning  
  - Refer to epilepsy specialist nurse or neurologist if: first seizure, diagnostic uncertainty, refractory epilepsy, or potential surgical candidate  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 80 mL/hr if nil by mouth (e.g. post-ictal) – avoid glucose solutions in low Na+ patients (risk of hyponatraemia exacerbation)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless contraindicated) – no interaction with most AEDs except enzyme-inducers may reduce LMWH levels? Uncertain, but still standard  
  - Diet: NBM until safe swallow assessed post-ictal; then soft diet  
  - Observations: 2-hourly for first 24h (GCS, vital signs, seizure chart)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (monotherapy initiation – refer to local titration schedules):**  
  - Lamotrigine: start 25 mg once daily, increase slowly (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome) – typical maintenance 200–400 mg/day in two divided doses. **Check local policy** for exact titration.  
  - Levetiracetam: start 250–500 mg twice daily, increase by 500 mg/day every 2 weeks – maintenance 1–3 g/day.  
  - Valproate: start 500 mg once daily (modified release) or 15 mg/kg/day – maintenance 1–2 g/day. **Contraindicated** in women of childbearing potential unless under pregnancy prevention programme.  
  - Carbamazepine: start 100 mg twice daily, increase slowly – maintenance 400–1200 mg/day (modified release). Induces own metabolism – monitor levels.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Focal: oxcarbazepine, brivaracetam, lacosamide  
  - Generalised: topiramate (weight loss), zonisamide, clobazam (add-on)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Lamotrigine + valproate: valproate doubles lamotrigine half-life → risk of toxicity; initial lamotrigine dose must be halved.  
  - Oral contraception + lamotrigine/carbamazepine: oestrogen reduces lamotrigine levels; enzyme-inducers reduce COC efficacy – use barrier method or higher oestrogen dose.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Levetiracetam: dose reduce in renal impairment (CrCl <80 mL/min) – see BNF.  
  - Valproate – caution in hepatic disease; avoid if active liver disease or family history of mitochondrial disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Lamotrigine rapid titration** – prescribing a loading dose or doubling doses too quickly causes severe, potentially fatal Stevens-Johnson syndrome. **Always follow local titration protocol.**  
  **Valproate in pregnancy** – known teratogen; if prescribed in woman of childbearing age, must have documented pregnancy prevention plan.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Status epilepticus (if subtherapeutic levels)  
  - Drug toxicity: phenytoin (nystagmus, ataxia), carbamazepine (blurred vision, hyponatraemia), lamotrigine (rash)  
  - Rash with lamotrigine or carbamazepine – stop immediately if blistering or mucosal involvement (SJS/TEN)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Bone demineralisation (enzyme-inducing AEDs) – check vitamin D, calcium; supplement if low  
  - Cognitive impairment (“brain fog”) – more with topiramate, zonisamide  
  - Weight gain (valproate, pregabalin, gabapentin)  
  - Mood changes – levetiracetam associated with aggression/depression; valproate with polycystic ovary syndrome  
  - SUDEP (sudden unexpected death in epilepsy) – risk increased with frequent GTC seizures, poor adherence, night-time seizures, young adult males
sections.Prognosis:
- **~70%** become seizure-free with first-line ASM (about 50% with first agent, additional 20% with second).  
- **~15%** need polytherapy; **~15%** have refractory epilepsy (fail to respond to ≥2 appropriate AEDs) – consider tertiary referral for surgical workup.  
- Mortality is increased 2–3x compared to general population, mainly due to SUDEP, accidents, and suicide.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check pregnancy test** and discuss contraception in every woman of childbearing age before starting any AED, especially valproate.  
- **Titrate lamotrigine slowly** (week 1: 25 mg daily, week 2: 25 mg twice daily, then increments every 2 weeks) – reduce starting dose if also on valproate.  
- **Counsel all patients about SUDEP** – reinforce medication adherence, avoidance of alcohol/sleep deprivation, and review of night-time seizure risk (consider nocturnal seizure monitor).  
- **Check AED levels at steady state** (after 5 half-lives) if adherence concern or toxicity suspected.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start lamotrigine at 100 mg daily** – provokes rash. Always follow slow titration.  
- **Do NOT abruptly stop any ASM** – causes withdrawal seizures; if switching, overlap gradually while tapering old drug.  
- **Do NOT prescribe valproate in a woman of childbearing age** without robust documentation of pregnancy prevention and signed annual review form (UK MHRA restrictions).  
- **Do NOT ignore drug interactions** – especially with OCP, antibiotics, and anticoagulants (enzyme-inducers reduce warfarin/DOAC efficacy).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Seizure recurrence >2 in 24 hours, prolonged seizure >5 min, or injury from seizure  
  - New rash (especially widespread, blistering, or mucosal involvement)  
  - Signs of mood change: new depression, aggression, suicidal thoughts (especially on levetiracetam)  
  - Signs of toxicity: severe dizziness, double vision, unsteady gait, confusion  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Epilepsy specialist nurse or neurology clinic **within 2–4 weeks** for drug titration review and side-effect monitoring.  
  - If newly diagnosed, urgent referral to first seizure clinic / neurology.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team immediately for:**  
  - **Status epilepticus** (seizure >5 min or 2 without recovery) – start IV lorazepam, call anaesthetics.  
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or rapidly rising – consider failed ABC, post-ictal respiratory depression, aspiration.  
  - **GCS ≤8** after 30 min of seizure termination – airway support needed.  
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (seizure-related, but if persists may indicate sepsis or rhabdomyolysis).  
  - **Suspected AED toxicity with encephalopathy** – consider Naloxone? No, specific antidotes: flumazenil (benzodiazepines) but avoid unless definite overdose; for phenytoin toxicity, supportive care.  
- **When to call epilepsy consultant:** refractory status epilepticus (failed 2 lines of therapy), first episode of status, or consideration of anaesthetic induction.

rawMarkdown:
## Epilepsy — Chronic Management  
**One-liner:** Chronic epilepsy management in adults – selecting & monitoring antiseizure medications (ASMs) to achieve seizure freedom while minimising adverse effects and risks.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?  
- **Status epilepticus** – prolonged seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery. **Key finding:** ongoing convulsive activity or unresponsive after 10 min → ABC, IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once after 10 min), call senior anaesthetics/ICU.  
- **Abrupt AED withdrawal** – sudden cessation of any ASM (especially benzodiazepines, phenytoin, valproate) can precipitate life-threatening seizures. **Key finding:** patient reports stopping medication in last 48–72 hours, with low or absent drug levels.  
- **AED toxicity** – e.g. phenytoin overdose causing ataxia, nystagmus, encephalopathy → check drug levels urgently; may need dose reduction or charcoal if massive overdose.  

### Presentation  
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Recurrent, unprovoked seizures (focal aware/impaired awareness, generalised tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic)  
  - Breakthrough seizures despite therapy (trigger: missed doses, sleep deprivation, illness, new drug interaction)  
  - Side effects of current AED: sedation, dizziness, tremor, rash, weight gain, mood changes  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Post-ictal confusion, Todd’s paresis, tongue or cheek lacerations, urinary incontinence  
  - In between seizures: usually normal neuro exam; may have subtle bradypsychia or ataxia from AEDs  

### Differential Diagnosis  
- **Most likely:**  
  - Epilepsy (multiple seizure types, EEG/MRI supportive)  
  - Syncope (brief loss of consciousness, injury rare, no post-ictal confusion, usually provoked)  
- **Less likely:**  
  - Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) – sustained eye closure, pelvic thrusting, no post-ictal state, no EEG correlate  
  - Transient ischaemic attack (TIA) – negative symptoms (weakness/numbness), no rhythmic jerking, no loss of consciousness  
  - Arrhythmia (e.g. long QT, Wolff-Parkinson-White) – syncope during exercise, family history, ECG abnormal  
- **Splitter:** Tongue bite (epilepsy vs. PNES) – lateral tongue bite >2mm strongly suggests true convulsion; PNES rarely causes bite.

### Investigations  
- **Bedside:**  
  - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimic)  
  - ECG (arrhythmia if syncope suspect)  
  - Urine pregnancy test (if woman of childbearing age – mandatory before starting valproate)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, Ca/Mg/PO4 (electrolyte derangement)  
  - AED levels (only if toxicity or adherence concern – check trough levels for phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate)  
  - ESR/CRP (if suspect CNS infection)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** MRI brain (epilepsy protocol – detects hippocampal sclerosis, tumours, malformations)  
  - **Second-line:** CT head (if MRI contraindicated or acute trauma/haemorrhage suspected)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - EEG (outpatient, ideally within 24–72 hours of seizure – shows interictal epileptiform discharges)  
  - Prolonged video-EEG if uncertain diagnosis or surgical candidacy  
- **Never-miss test:** **AED level** (before changing dose) + **pregnancy test** (in all women of childbearing age starting or changing AED, especially valproate)

### Management  
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If active seizure: ABC, IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once if still seizing after 10 min); if ongoing → phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV or levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV [check local policy]  
  - Identify and treat underlying trigger: hypoglycaemia, infections, drug withdrawal, missed doses  
  - Check AED levels (if known drug, e.g. phenytoin)  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Reconcile all ASMs – ensure same drug, dose, interval as outpatient (or adjust if toxicity)  
  - Establish seizure diary and triggers (sleep, alcohol, missed doses)  
  - Start new ASM if treatment-naïve or switching: **first-line monotherapy**  
    - Focal seizures: lamotrigine (slow titration to reduce rash risk) or levetiracetam  
    - Generalised tonic-clonic: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or valproate (avoid women of childbearing potential)  
    - Absence: ethosuximide or valproate  
    - Myoclonic: valproate or levetiracetam  
  - Check baseline LFTs, FBC, renal function  
  - Educate patient/carer: medication adherence, driving regulations (DVLA: must stop driving for 12 months after first seizure or 6 months if controlled), SUDEP risk counselling  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Titrate to maintenance dose over weeks (see titration schedules in BNF/Antiepileptic drug guidelines)  
  - Monitor for adverse effects: rash (lamotrigine), behavioural change (levetiracetam), weight gain (valproate, gabapentin), osteoporosis (enzyme-inducers: carbamazepine, phenytoin)  
  - Annual review: seizure control, side effects, blood tests if on enzyme-inducing AED (bone profile, vitamin D)  
  - Consider drug level monitoring if non-adherence, toxicity risk, or pregnancy planning  
  - Refer to epilepsy specialist nurse or neurologist if: first seizure, diagnostic uncertainty, refractory epilepsy, or potential surgical candidate  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 80 mL/hr if nil by mouth (e.g. post-ictal) – avoid glucose solutions in low Na+ patients (risk of hyponatraemia exacerbation)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless contraindicated) – no interaction with most AEDs except enzyme-inducers may reduce LMWH levels? Uncertain, but still standard  
  - Diet: NBM until safe swallow assessed post-ictal; then soft diet  
  - Observations: 2-hourly for first 24h (GCS, vital signs, seizure chart)  

### Drugs & Doses  
- **First-line agents (monotherapy initiation – refer to local titration schedules):**  
  - Lamotrigine: start 25 mg once daily, increase slowly (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome) – typical maintenance 200–400 mg/day in two divided doses. **Check local policy** for exact titration.  
  - Levetiracetam: start 250–500 mg twice daily, increase by 500 mg/day every 2 weeks – maintenance 1–3 g/day.  
  - Valproate: start 500 mg once daily (modified release) or 15 mg/kg/day – maintenance 1–2 g/day. **Contraindicated** in women of childbearing potential unless under pregnancy prevention programme.  
  - Carbamazepine: start 100 mg twice daily, increase slowly – maintenance 400–1200 mg/day (modified release). Induces own metabolism – monitor levels.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Focal: oxcarbazepine, brivaracetam, lacosamide  
  - Generalised: topiramate (weight loss), zonisamide, clobazam (add-on)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Lamotrigine + valproate: valproate doubles lamotrigine half-life → risk of toxicity; initial lamotrigine dose must be halved.  
  - Oral contraception + lamotrigine/carbamazepine: oestrogen reduces lamotrigine levels; enzyme-inducers reduce COC efficacy – use barrier method or higher oestrogen dose.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Levetiracetam: dose reduce in renal impairment (CrCl <80 mL/min) – see BNF.  
  - Valproate – caution in hepatic disease; avoid if active liver disease or family history of mitochondrial disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Lamotrigine rapid titration** – prescribing a loading dose or doubling doses too quickly causes severe, potentially fatal Stevens-Johnson syndrome. **Always follow local titration protocol.**  
  **Valproate in pregnancy** – known teratogen; if prescribed in woman of childbearing age, must have documented pregnancy prevention plan.

### Complications  
- **Acute:**  
  - Status epilepticus (if subtherapeutic levels)  
  - Drug toxicity: phenytoin (nystagmus, ataxia), carbamazepine (blurred vision, hyponatraemia), lamotrigine (rash)  
  - Rash with lamotrigine or carbamazepine – stop immediately if blistering or mucosal involvement (SJS/TEN)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Bone demineralisation (enzyme-inducing AEDs) – check vitamin D, calcium; supplement if low  
  - Cognitive impairment (“brain fog”) – more with topiramate, zonisamide  
  - Weight gain (valproate, pregabalin, gabapentin)  
  - Mood changes – levetiracetam associated with aggression/depression; valproate with polycystic ovary syndrome  
  - SUDEP (sudden unexpected death in epilepsy) – risk increased with frequent GTC seizures, poor adherence, night-time seizures, young adult males  

### Prognosis  
- **~70%** become seizure-free with first-line ASM (about 50% with first agent, additional 20% with second).  
- **~15%** need polytherapy; **~15%** have refractory epilepsy (fail to respond to ≥2 appropriate AEDs) – consider tertiary referral for surgical workup.  
- Mortality is increased 2–3x compared to general population, mainly due to SUDEP, accidents, and suicide.

### Pearls (positive actions)  
- **Always check pregnancy test** and discuss contraception in every woman of childbearing age before starting any AED, especially valproate.  
- **Titrate lamotrigine slowly** (week 1: 25 mg daily, week 2: 25 mg twice daily, then increments every 2 weeks) – reduce starting dose if also on valproate.  
- **Counsel all patients about SUDEP** – reinforce medication adherence, avoidance of alcohol/sleep deprivation, and review of night-time seizure risk (consider nocturnal seizure monitor).  
- **Check AED levels at steady state** (after 5 half-lives) if adherence concern or toxicity suspected.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)  
- **Do NOT start lamotrigine at 100 mg daily** – provokes rash. Always follow slow titration.  
- **Do NOT abruptly stop any ASM** – causes withdrawal seizures; if switching, overlap gradually while tapering old drug.  
- **Do NOT prescribe valproate in a woman of childbearing age** without robust documentation of pregnancy prevention and signed annual review form (UK MHRA restrictions).  
- **Do NOT ignore drug interactions** – especially with OCP, antibiotics, and anticoagulants (enzyme-inducers reduce warfarin/DOAC efficacy).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  
- **Return if:**  
  - Seizure recurrence >2 in 24 hours, prolonged seizure >5 min, or injury from seizure  
  - New rash (especially widespread, blistering, or mucosal involvement)  
  - Signs of mood change: new depression, aggression, suicidal thoughts (especially on levetiracetam)  
  - Signs of toxicity: severe dizziness, double vision, unsteady gait, confusion  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Epilepsy specialist nurse or neurology clinic **within 2–4 weeks** for drug titration review and side-effect monitoring.  
  - If newly diagnosed, urgent referral to first seizure clinic / neurology.

### Escalation Criteria  
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team immediately for:**  
  - **Status epilepticus** (seizure >5 min or 2 without recovery) – start IV lorazepam, call anaesthetics.  
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or rapidly rising – consider failed ABC, post-ictal respiratory depression, aspiration.  
  - **GCS ≤8** after 30 min of seizure termination – airway support needed.  
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (seizure-related, but if persists may indicate sepsis or rhabdomyolysis).  
  - **Suspected AED toxicity with encephalopathy** – consider Naloxone? No, specific antidotes: flumazenil (benzodiazepines) but avoid unless definite overdose; for phenytoin toxicity, supportive care.  
- **When to call epilepsy consultant:** refractory status epilepticus (failed 2 lines of therapy), first episode of status, or consideration of anaesthetic induction.
